dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 responding for breathing problems at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY]. Report chief complaint and patient condition.
Hi ma'am, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain, extreme shortness of breath for about 30 minutes. I'm using a nebulizer but no relief.
Can you tell me your name please?
I'm, uh, Sarah.
What city are we in right now, Sarah?
I think... [CITY].
What day is today, Sarah? Can you tell me the date?
July... uh, 30th.
What happened today, Sarah? Can you tell me briefly?
I had trouble breathing, used nebulizer, no help. Got very worse. That's why I called 911.
Okay. You're alert. Any other difficulties besides the breathing?
No, just really bad breath trouble. I can barely talk.
Sarah, we're going to do a few things now, okay? First, can we look at your airway?
Okay, whatever's necessary. I can't breathe well, you know.
Airway is patent. Positioning suggests tripod consistent with breathing distress.
Sarah, I'm going to gently reposition your head. It might help. Ready for that?
Yeah, please do it. I feel like I'm failing already.
Airway is patent with clear workspace for assisted ventilation if needed.
No jugular venous distension noted.
Resp rate as high as 30 initially. Equal chest rise and fall. Wheeze bilaterally on auscultation. Oxygen saturation at 96%.
Sarah, just for a moment, let me hear your breathing more clearly. This will help us make a better assessment.
I'll try. (deep breath effort) In... Out. It's really working hard. Lungs wheeze both in and out, I feel it!

Concordant with bilateral expiratory and inspiratory wheeze. Equal rise and fall. High work of breathing consistent with COPD/asthma exacerbation. No apnea observed. Pulse ox at 96%. BVM is ready if needed.
Glasgow coma score is 15 (Eye = 4, Verbal = 5, Motor = 6), indicating the patient is alert.
Heart rate is 124, strong and palpable. Systolic was 162, diastolic 100. Skin is diaphoretic, clammy, and flushed.
Sarah, can you tell me more about your chest discomfort? Where is it located and how would you rate its severity out of 10?
Sarah, can you tell me what medications you are currently using?
I take Albuterol with nebulizer, Zofran for nausea, acetaminophen, Motrin, ibuprofen, and Percocet.
Do you have a medical history we should know about before transport?
Yes, I have asthma and COPD. I've used BiPAP before for flare-ups.
Do you have any known allergies to medications or other substances?
No known drug allergies.
Sarah's chief complaint is severe shortness of breath with chest pain from a suspected asthma/COPD exacerbation. This is the primary focus of care.
Sarah, I know you're in a lot of stress, but let's focus on your chest discomfort now. When did it begin, and how long has it lasted?
It started at the same time as my breathing problem, more than 30 minutes now. It's constant and not improving at all.
On our scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst you've ever had, how would you rate your chest pain now?
Eight. It gets worse when I work hard to breathe.
What does it feel like? Is it constant or does it come and go?
It's sharp and tight, constant, mostly during breathing. It doesn't spread outward.
Does the pain radiate to your arms, neck, or jaw?
No, it's right here in the middle of my chest.
Does any position or breathing pattern help, or make it worse?
No position helps. The nebulizer makes no difference. It just stays bad.
Exactly how long have you had the chest pain and breathing problems today?
Over 30 minutes, and it's been going on continuously ever since.
Skin is diaphoretic, clammy, and flushed.
Head and neck show no deformity or swelling.
Nasal passages are clear. No signs of any obstruction.
Echoed wheezing consistent with previous assessment. Chest rise and fall is even.
Cardiac rhythm stable for now.
Peripheral pulses are strong and regular. No signs of injury.
Blood pressure is 162/100.
Heart rate is 124.
Respiratory rate is 30.
Oxygen saturation is 96%.
Glucose reading is 0.
EKG findings show sinus tachycardia with nonspecific T-wave abnormalities and a prolonged QT interval.
Attempted IV start in the arm. Unable to secure.
Duoneb administration-Albuterol 5mg and Ipratropium 0.5mg-via nebulizer as per standing order. Oxygen 15 LPM via non-rebreather initiated.
Dexamethasone 10mg administered intramuscularly. This can assist with reducing airway inflammation.
Manual jaw thrust performed to maintain a patent airway with the patient's heavy respiratory effort.
Initiating Protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress/asthma/COPD.
Reassessments at 09:27:56 show heart rate 117, respiratory rate 21, blood pressure 174/80, saturation 94%. No acute decline or improvement observed.
Repeated 4-lead and 12-lead EKG to monitor any changes in cardiac rhythm-no new findings detected.
Reconfirmed wheezing on auscultation, patient still in significant distress with pain assessing consistently at 8 out of 10.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] with pre-advance notice to ED. Maintaining close monitoring during transit.
Applying 5-point seat belt harness to the stretcher in preparation for transportation.
Safety check completed. Patient is secured for ambulance loading.
Patient is loaded into ambulance. Transport initiated.
Patient has become unresponsive and is now in full respiratory arrest. Immediate BVM ventilation initiated while maintaining secured transport protocols.
Vitals will be rechecked every 2 minutes during transport.
Vitals are maintained at 174/80, 117, 21, 94% saturation, and EKG persists with sinus tach.
Non-rebreather provides 15 LPM oxygen. Monitored every 5 minutes.
Another nebulizer dose of Albuterol and Atrovent is prepped in case of no improvement.
No change in presentation-still in severe distress with no sign of reactivity to treatment.
Second dose of Albuterol/Atrovent via nebulizer per standing order administered.
No shift in alertness. GCS remains at 15. Skin is still diaphoretic with stable color observed.
BP at 200/140 with respiratory rate of 12-consistent with the ePCR.
IV attempt unsuccessful first time. Prepping for a second attempt.
Second IV attempt in another extremity-no success yet.
Dexamethasone administered and respiratory status being monitored for the next 5 minutes.
No improvement-ventilation demands unchanged.
Patient becomes unresponsive. Switching to BVM immediately.
Continuing airway management with manual jaw thrust and observing chest rise.
Re-alerting ED of ongoing respiratory support, vitals, and BVM use.
ED nurses have been notified. Prepared for drop into ER.
ePCR documentation complete. No patient signature due to unconsciousness.
Contact method arranged with primary caregiver during registration.
All relevant patient information ready for handover to ED.
Patient also regularly takes oxycodone for pain management at home.
Transporting to ED. All equipment verified and functioning.
